title: Koko Eating Bananas
code: public static int MinEatingSpeed(int[] piles, int h)
foreach (int p in piles) hi = Math.Max(hi, p);

frames.caption: Answer space = eating speeds [1, 11] (hi = max(piles)). Find the minimum feasible speed.
frames.note: There is no sorted input to search here. Instead we search the SPACE OF POSSIBLE ANSWERS — the eating speeds [1, max(piles)] — using a monotone feasibility predicate. Speed 1 is the slowest sensible rate; a speed equal to the largest pile (11) already eats every pile in one hour, so faster cannot help. That fixes the bounds at lo = 1, hi = 11.

frames.caption: hoursNeeded(k) = ceil(3/k)+ceil(6/k)+ceil(7/k)+ceil(11/k) decreases as k grows — predicate is monotone.
frames.note: The whole trick is monotonicity: hoursNeeded(k) = sum(ceil(pile / k)) only DECREASES as k grows, so isFeasible(k) = hoursNeeded(k) <= h flips from false…false to true…true exactly once. That single flip is the ordered yes/no test binary search needs; without it, halving the range would be meaningless.

frames.caption: mid = 1 + (11 - 1)/2 = 6. Test eating speed k = 6.
frames.note: Half-open lower bound: mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, computed this way (not (lo + hi) / 2) to avoid 32-bit overflow. We are hunting the FIRST feasible speed, so the loop is lo < hi with hi = mid (keep mid, it might be the answer) rather than the closed-interval mid +/- 1 used for exact-match search.

frames.caption: hoursNeeded(6) = 1+1+2+2 = 6 <= 8: feasible.
frames.note: Feasibility is an O(n) scan: hours += ceil(pile / speed), done with integer math as (pile + speed - 1) / speed to dodge floating point. This is the O(k) cost per probe that makes Koko O(n log(max(piles))) overall — a log number of probes, each an O(n) sweep.

frames.caption: Feasible -> keep mid as a candidate and try slower: hi = mid = 6.
frames.note: hoursNeeded(mid) <= h: this speed finishes in time. Because slower-but-still-feasible speeds may exist, we do NOT return — we set hi = mid to keep mid as a candidate and try to go SLOWER. Lower bound never discards the current feasible value, only the strictly-larger half above it.

frames.caption: mid = 1 + (6 - 1)/2 = 3. Test eating speed k = 3.
frames.note: Half-open lower bound: mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, computed this way (not (lo + hi) / 2) to avoid 32-bit overflow. We are hunting the FIRST feasible speed, so the loop is lo < hi with hi = mid (keep mid, it might be the answer) rather than the closed-interval mid +/- 1 used for exact-match search.

frames.caption: hoursNeeded(3) = 1+2+3+4 = 10 > 8: too slow.
frames.note: Feasibility is an O(n) scan: hours += ceil(pile / speed), done with integer math as (pile + speed - 1) / speed to dodge floating point. This is the O(k) cost per probe that makes Koko O(n log(max(piles))) overall — a log number of probes, each an O(n) sweep.

frames.caption: Too slow -> discard speeds <= 3: lo = mid + 1 = 4.
frames.note: hoursNeeded(mid) > h: too slow, Koko runs out of hours. Every speed at or below mid is also too slow (monotonicity), so discard that whole half: lo = mid + 1. We must go FASTER. This is the false…false part of the predicate collapsing away.

frames.caption: mid = 4 + (6 - 4)/2 = 5. Test eating speed k = 5.
frames.note: Half-open lower bound: mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, computed this way (not (lo + hi) / 2) to avoid 32-bit overflow. We are hunting the FIRST feasible speed, so the loop is lo < hi with hi = mid (keep mid, it might be the answer) rather than the closed-interval mid +/- 1 used for exact-match search.

frames.caption: hoursNeeded(5) = 1+2+2+3 = 8 <= 8: feasible.
frames.note: Feasibility is an O(n) scan: hours += ceil(pile / speed), done with integer math as (pile + speed - 1) / speed to dodge floating point. This is the O(k) cost per probe that makes Koko O(n log(max(piles))) overall — a log number of probes, each an O(n) sweep.

frames.caption: Feasible -> keep mid as a candidate and try slower: hi = mid = 5.
frames.note: hoursNeeded(mid) <= h: this speed finishes in time. Because slower-but-still-feasible speeds may exist, we do NOT return — we set hi = mid to keep mid as a candidate and try to go SLOWER. Lower bound never discards the current feasible value, only the strictly-larger half above it.

frames.caption: mid = 4 + (5 - 4)/2 = 4. Test eating speed k = 4.
frames.note: Half-open lower bound: mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, computed this way (not (lo + hi) / 2) to avoid 32-bit overflow. We are hunting the FIRST feasible speed, so the loop is lo < hi with hi = mid (keep mid, it might be the answer) rather than the closed-interval mid +/- 1 used for exact-match search.

frames.caption: hoursNeeded(4) = 1+2+2+3 = 8 <= 8: feasible.
frames.note: Feasibility is an O(n) scan: hours += ceil(pile / speed), done with integer math as (pile + speed - 1) / speed to dodge floating point. This is the O(k) cost per probe that makes Koko O(n log(max(piles))) overall — a log number of probes, each an O(n) sweep.

frames.caption: Feasible -> keep mid as a candidate and try slower: hi = mid = 4.
frames.note: hoursNeeded(mid) <= h: this speed finishes in time. Because slower-but-still-feasible speeds may exist, we do NOT return — we set hi = mid to keep mid as a candidate and try to go SLOWER. Lower bound never discards the current feasible value, only the strictly-larger half above it.

frames.caption: lo == hi = 4: the interval collapsed. Minimum feasible eating speed = 4.
frames.note: lo == hi: the interval has collapsed to a single value, and by the invariant it is the smallest speed for which the predicate is true. That is the minimum feasible eating speed. The range [1, 11] was pinned to 4 in just four probes — no sorting, only the monotone predicate.
